coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped porous hollow carbon nanospheres with a high nitrogen content: A sustainable synthetic strategy using energetic precursors
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Peng
Jiao, Qingjie
Ren, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Improving the recovery and utilization efficiency of obsolete energetic materials (EMs) is essential for addressing environmental pollution. In this sense, a sustainable one-step high-temperature carbonization strategy using 2,2′,4,4′,6,6′-hexanitrostilbene-based (HNS-based) energetic hollow nanospheres as energetic precursors was used to fabricate nitrogen-doped (N-doped) porous hollow carbon nanospheres with a high N content. The experimental results suggested carbon-based materials with a hollow spherical framework nanostructure can be obtained by the high-temperature carbonization of energetic precursors. The obtained samples possessed N-doped contents of 19.54 wt% at the carbonization temperature of 600 °C and even 6.10 wt% at 900 °C. In addition, hollow carbon nanospheres with a large number of hierarchical pores and a high surface area (503.5 m2/g) were produced at 900 °C. This strategy prevented unnecessary safety risks and improved recovery and utilization efficiency in a more sustainable and economic manner than conventional disposal methods of EMs. Therefore, this work provides a proof-of-principle concept for the fabrication of carbon-based fundamental functional materials from obsolete EMs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energetic precursor
High-temperature carbonization
Sustainable strategy
Carbon nanospheres
High nitrogen content

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) ( 21975227 and 21975024 ). We thank BL07W in NSRL and Prof. Liang Chen (University of science and technology of China, Hefei, China), Prof. Yu Liu and Dr. Jinjiang Xu (Institute of chemical materials, China academy of engineering physics, Mianyang, China) for support and help.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structures of (a) HNS, (b) piperazine and (c) HEHSs.
FT-IR spectra of (a) energetic precursor HEHSs, (b) N-PHCS-600, (c) N-PHCS-700, (d) N-PHCS-800 and (e) N-PHCS-900.
TG-DTG curve of energetic precursor HEHSs.
SEM images of N-PHCS after carbonization at (a) 600, (b) 700, (c) 800 and (d) 900 °C.
TEM images of N-PHCS after carbonization at (a) 600, (b) 700, (c) 800 and (d) 900 °C.
3D reconstruction microtomography image of N-PHCS-900 derived from X-ray microtomography data.
High-resolution N1s X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of N-PHCS carbonized at (a) 600, (b) 700, (c) 800 and (d) 900 °C.
BET surface area and elemental analysis results of N-PHCS at different temperatures.
Purpose, method and safety risk of EMs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: High: high risk exists in the detonation and deflagration process in the form of high temperature and pressure.
Moderate: small risk exists in this process, but the experimental conditions need to be controlled strictly.
Low: nearly no risk exists in this process.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based materials with different N-doped contents.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped porous hollow carbon nanospheres with a high nitrogen content: A sustainable synthetic strategy using energetic precursors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Explosion Science and Technology, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Explosion Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Explosion Science and Technology, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Improving the recovery and utilization efficiency of obsolete energetic materials (EMs) is essential for addressing environmental pollution. In this sense, a sustainable one-step high-temperature carbonization strategy using 2,2′,4,4′,6,6′-hexanitrostilbene-based (HNS-based) energetic hollow nanospheres as energetic precursors was used to fabricate nitrogen-doped (N-doped) porous hollow carbon nanospheres with a high N content. The experimental results suggested carbon-based materials with a hollow spherical framework nanostructure can be obtained by the high-temperature carbonization of energetic precursors. The obtained samples possessed N-doped contents of 19.54 wt% at the carbonization temperature of 600 °C and even 6.10 wt% at 900 °C. In addition, hollow carbon nanospheres with a large number of hierarchical pores and a high surface area (503.5 m2/g) were produced at 900 °C. This strategy prevented unnecessary safety risks and improved recovery and utilization efficiency in a more sustainable and economic manner than conventional disposal methods of EMs. Therefore, this work provides a proof-of-principle concept for the fabrication of carbon-based fundamental functional materials from obsolete EMs.

ce:section.ce:para: Energetic materials (EMs), an important class of chemical energy compounds (Li et al., 2019b; Deng et al., 2019a, 2019c; Sun et al., 2018), are used widely as a primary component in both military and civilian applications. However, EMs must be retired when their service times expire (Arora et al., 2018; Ferreira et al., 2015). They are considered extremely dangerous chemical hazards until their retirement, providing a serious threat to and impact on human beings and the environment (Cybulska et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018). Follow-up management strategies for addressing these issues have received a significant amount of attention from many researchers with diverse areas of expertise.

However, current treatments are relatively homogeneous (Thouin et al. 2018 and 2019; Martin et al., 2013). Destruction, which primarily involves burning and degradation (El-Diwani et al., 2009; Fuller et al., 2019), is currently regarded as an important and effective method for processing obsolete EMs. However, this method involves the unavoidable waste of chemical resources and serious direct economic loss because of the high cost of raw chemical resources (Alverbro et al., 2009). Because burning is used in the destruction of EMs, safety is also a concern. These mishandled risks could lead to a significant amount of unnecessary economic loss, directly affecting human beings and their society and indirectly affecting the ecological environment (Albright, 2012; Gwenzi et al., 2018). Therefore, developing a novel green, clean, and sustainable technology is urgent and essential for the disposal of obsolete EMs.

Recycling and utilization technology has been proposed as a promising strategy to avoid waste and to reuse the chemical resources contained in obsolete EMs (Liu et al., 2015b). Researchers agree that the conversion of obsolete EMs into available fundamental functional materials is important for use in various applications (Lu et al., 2005; Vlasov et al., 2010; Li et al., 2019a, 2019b) because of the inherent characteristics of EMs, such as their strong combustion and detonation performances, rapid reaction properties and high carbon and nitrogen contents, among other characteristics. However, the small number of methods for the conversion of EMs into available fundamental functional materials cannot effectively meet the large disposal demand because of the intrinsic hazardous properties of EMs. Especially during deflagration (Liu et al., 2015b), detonation (Lu et al., 2005) and high-temperature physical vapor deposition processes (Li et al., 2018), the number of risk factors increases as control decreases. In addition, it is also difficult to promote the broadened transformation from EMs into fundamental functional materials on a large scale. Thus, serious challenges in the recovery and utilization of obsolete EMs still remained.

Recently, an interesting self-assembly reaction was discovered in our previous study: 2,2′,4,4′,6,6′-hexanitrostilbene (HNS),which was regarded as an important second explosive in the fields of aerospace, aviation, weapons and ammunition, was reacted with 1,4-diazacyclohexane (piperazine), forming an energetic hollow nanosphere-based material with a high colloid stability (Cao et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2018). Unexpectedly, the poor thermal decomposition performance of this material was indicative of a slow exothermic and incomplete decomposition process. In addition, these carbonized products remained in the form of stable hollow carbon nanospheres, thus demonstrating a novel method for fabricating hollow carbon nanosphere-based fundamental functional materials using EMs as precursors.

Hollow carbon nanospheres, as a class of important fundamental functional materials, have attracted much attention in many fields, such as energy, adsorption, catalysis and so on (Inagaki et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2015; Peng et al., 2017). Especially, hollow carbon nanospheres with high nitrogen contents and high porosities have good performance in terms of its functionalized applications, owing to tailored energy bands from heterogeneous elements-doped structure, as well as more ion channels and reaction spaces from unique micro/nano pore structures (Liu et al., 2015a). Conventional synthesis methods often involved more complicated process conditions to prepare hollow carbon nanospheres. And more heterogeneous elements sources and additives were added into the fabrication stages to regulate and control the heterogeneous element-doped contents and their porosities, resulting in large consumption of resources and environmental pollution (Feng et al., 2014; Inagaki et al., 2018). It is therefore urgent and necessary to develop a novel and green strategy to design and fabricate hollow carbon nanospheres.

In this work, a one-step sustainable high-temperature carbonization strategy using HNS-based energetic hollow nanospheres (HEHSs) as energetic precursors was designed and employed for the synthesis of N-doped porous hollow carbon nanospheres (N-PHCS) with a high N content. Variation in the types of fabricated structural features with the carbonization temperature was investigated. The differences between and advantages of this strategy over previously reported methods were evaluated by analyzing the safety risks and economic benefits of both methods.

Raw HNS materials were provided by Institute of chemical materials, China academy of engineering physics (Institute of Chemical Materials, China Academy of Engineering Physics). Commercial Piperazine (99.9%, C4H10N2) was Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. HEHSs were synthesized via a co-polymerization reaction. Briefly, 0.01 mol HNS and 0.045 mol piperazine were added to a 250-ml flask containing 180 ml acetonitrile. The mixture was placed in an 80-°C water bath and agitated at 150 rpm for 5 h. After allowing the mixture to cool to room temperature overnight, the reaction product (HEHSs) was obtained after vacuum filtration. The chemical structures of the reactants and product are presented in Fig. 1 .

A corundum boat loaded with 1.0 g HEHSs was placed at the central section of the tube furnace. The tube furnace was purged using high-purity argon (Ar) several times. Subsequently, the temperature of the furnace was increased at a heating rate of 5 °C/min until the final carbonization temperature was reached. The final temperature was held for 3 h, and constant Ar gas flow was maintained. Finally, N-PHCS were obtained immediately after the furnace was allowed to cool to room temperature. The studied carbonization temperatures were 600, 700, 800 and 900 °C, which corresponded to the following samples: N-PHCS-600, N-PHCS-700, N-PHCS-800 and N-PHCS-900, respectively.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and elemental mapping measurements were obtained using an Ultra 55 microscope (ZEISS, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected by a Libra 200 microscope (ZEISS, Germany) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Synchrotron radiation X-ray imaging technology was conducted at the BL07W beamline station at the National Synchrotron Radiation Light Source (NSRL), located in Hefei, China. A three-dimensional (3D) visualization of the tomograms was obtained using Avizo Fire VSG software (Visualization Sciences Group, France), and a simulation video of the sample morphology was obtained using the original video signal from a group of X-ray imaging technology images. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, USA) using Cu Kα irradiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet-5700 spectrometer using pressed KBr pellets over a wavelength range of 500–4000 cm− 1. 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (13C NMR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker Avance 600 instrument (Bruker, Switzerland). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Raman spectra were obtained using a Raman spectrophotometer (Renishaw, England). Elemental analysis was conducted using a Vario EL III elemental analyzer (Elementar, Germany). Thermogravimetric-derivative thermogravimetry (TG-DTG) curves were recorded using a STA449F3 thermal gravimetric analyzer (Netzsch, Germany) using a heating rate of 10 °C/min and the protection gas N2 of 50 ml/min. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were obtained at 77 K on a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 sorptometer. Samples were degassed at 100 °C for 12 h under high vacuum prior to measurement. The specific surface areas (SBET and SLangmuir) and pore size distributions (PSDs) were calculated with the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods, respectively, using the N2 adsorption isotherm data.

Energetic precursor HEHSs were synthesized via a co-polymerization method. HNS reacted with piperazine free radicals from CC bonds, forming HNS-based co-polymerization derivatives. Fig. 2a shows the HEHSs exhibited good monodispersity and highly regular micro/nanospherical shapes. Using statistics derived from the Nanomeasure data presented in Fig. S1, approximately 95% of the HEHSs were uniform nanospheres (average diameter: ~900 nm) with a relative standard deviation below 5%, demonstrating a narrow size distribution. Fig. 2b shows the unique structure of the nanospheres, i.e., a damaged nanoparticle revealed the fabricated HEHSs were hollow.

The XRD pattern of HEHSs in Fig. S2a reveals the HEHSs were amorphous materials (Cao et al., 2018), indicating HNS was completely converted into energetic amorphous derivatives via co-polymerization upon comparison with the HNS XRD pattern (JCPDS number: 00-042-1929) shown in Fig. S2b. The FT-IR spectrum of HEHSs in Fig. 3 shows two sharp peaks at 1532 and 1340 cm− 1, which correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes of the nitro groups present in the product. The 13C NMR spectrum is shown in Fig. S3. According to the previous literature (Yang et al., 2020; Deng et al., 2018), the resonance peaks ranged from 170 to 110 ppm, indicative of the presence of aromatic carbon, and a resonance peak was observed at 45.7 ppm, indicative of the carbon present in piperazine. As a whole, the characterization results demonstrated the energetic precursor HEHSs were prepared successfully.

Fig. 4 shows the HEHS TG-DTG curves exhibited a continuous and slow thermal decomposition loss weight process, which is unlike other high-energy-density explosives that can produce a rapid and full heat release in a very short period of time (Cao et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Deng et al., 2017; 2019b). The incomplete thermal weightlessness observed in the TG analysis indicated the mass loss of HEHSs was approximately 50.0% at 600 °C and 81.0% at 900 °C, demonstrating the carbonized products had been converted into carbonaceous residue. Fig. S4a shows similar hollow sphere nanostructures have remained intact in a high-temperature environment without fracturing or collapsing. In addition, Fig. S4b confirmed the hollow structure of the HEHS, despite a poorly deposited nanogold coating. HNS is widely used for military and civilian applications because of its excellent thermal and shock stabilities. However, few methods for its recovery and utilization currently exist. Herein, a possible sustainable strategy for fabricating carbon-based nanomaterials from HEHSs was investigated systematically.

N-PHCS were prepared by a one-step high-temperature carbonization strategy using HEHSs as energetic precursors. Fig. 5 shows the as-prepared N-PHCS samples at different carbonization temperatures still retain a fundamental nanosphere framework that exhibits good uniformity and high sphericity. As the carbonization temperature increased, the HEHS organic skeleton decomposed, resulting in the formation of a carbonaceous framework. In addition, the framework decreased in size but did not collapse because of the strong intramolecular crosslinking properties of the HEHSs (Wang et al., 2016). The size of the N-PHCS were regulated and controlled accurately by changing the carbonization temperature. At 900 °C, N-PHCS-900 exhibited the greatest shrinkage: the average nanosphere diameter was <500 nm although the HEHS average diameter was 900 nm.

Fig. 6 shows TEM images of the N-PHCS; these images verify the highly spherical morphology and excellent monodispersity of the nanoparticles. In addition, the difference in particle size between N-PHCS-600 (~800 nm, in Fig. 6a) and N-PHCS-900 (~500 nm, in Fig. 6d) was indicative of continuous shrinking as temperature increased. Contrast differences observed for the N-PHCS, shown in Fig. 6, suggested the varying degrees of weightlessness obtained via carbonization at different temperatures agreed with the TG curve results, indicating the formation of a thin carbon-based framework at 900 °C, as shown in Fig. 6d.

However, the TEM images did not effectively depict the inner structure of the N-PHCS because of their thick shell. Furthermore, synchrotron radiation X-ray imaging technology, an advanced nanocomputed tomography technique (Song et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018), was used to examine the inner structure of the N-PHCS. During imaging, carbon-based frameworks absorbed a larger amount of X-rays than empty zones. Distinct differences in the gray value (imaging contrast) were observed when these two areas were compared. After choosing a suitable gray value threshold for distinguishing the two areas, the spatial distribution of the carbon-based framework of the N-PHCS was extracted. 3D simulation videos of the morphology of N-PHCS-600 and N-PHCS-900 samples are presented in Appendices B and C, and Fig. 7 shows a 3D reconstruction microtomography image of N-PHCS-900. The yellow shells and red cores suggest the N-PHCS had inner hollow, non-spherical cavities. These observations demonstrated N-PHCS prepared via high-temperature carbonization using HEHSs as energetic precursors produced a hollow sphere nanostructure.

However, the TEM images did not effectively depict the inner structure of the N-PHCS because of their thick shell. Furthermore, synchrotron radiation X-ray imaging technology, an advanced nanocomputed tomography technique (Song et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018), was used to examine the inner structure of the N-PHCS. During imaging, carbon-based frameworks absorbed a larger amount of X-rays than empty zones. Distinct differences in the gray value (imaging contrast) were observed when these two areas were compared. After choosing a suitable gray value threshold for distinguishing the two areas, the spatial distribution of the carbon-based framework of the N-PHCS was extracted. 3D simulation videos of the morphology of N-PHCS-600 and N-PHCS-900 samples are presented in Appendices B and C, and Fig. 7 shows a 3D reconstruction microtomography image of N-PHCS-900. The yellow shells and red cores suggest the N-PHCS had inner hollow, non-spherical cavities. These observations demonstrated N-PHCS prepared via high-temperature carbonization using HEHSs as energetic precursors produced a hollow sphere nanostructure.

The structure of the N-PHCS was further investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3, absorption peaks at surrounding 1320 and 1585 cm− 1 were observed and were attributed to vibrations from CN bond stretching and the surrounding aromatic framework. The absence of the sharp peaks of nitro groups, which were present in the HEHS spectrum, indicated unstable energetic functional groups decomposed easily when exposed to a high-temperature environment. The broad diffraction peak (002) at 25.4° and obscure diffraction peak (100) at 43.9° were observed in the N-PHCS XRD patterns shown in Fig. S5, indicating the presence of a disordered phase, i.e., an amorphous carbonaceous material. As shown in Fig. S6, two peaks were observed using Raman spectroscopy. These peaks were located at approximately 1360 and 1580 cm− 1 and were attributed to the D band (the breathing mode of the Κ-point phonons of A1g symmetry) and G band (the in-plane stretching motion of symmetric sp2 CC bonds). The ID:IG ratio of N-PHCS-900 was numerically equal to 1.016 and was higher than those of N-PHCS-600 (0.883), N-PHCS-700 (0.953) and N-PHCS-800 (0.976), suggesting the presence of a certain degree of disorder in the N-PHCS framework.

The N-PHCS (C, N and O) were also analyzed using selective regional elemental mapping. The results are presented in Figs. S7–S10. The most abundant primary element found in the N-PHCS was C, and a uniform distribution of heteroatoms in heteroatom-doped carbon-based materials was also observed. Different heteroatoms, including N and O, were doped into different hollow carbon nanosphere frameworks. A high N-doping content was predicted for the N-PHCS, which were fabricated from HEHSs with high C and N contents.

In addition, the N-doped N-PHCS were analyzed using XPS and elemental analysis. XPS was used to analyze the elemental surface composition of the N-PHCS; the obtained XPS spectra are presented in Fig. S11. Three peaks, C1s, N1s and O1s, were observed, confirming the coexistence of N and O in the carbonaceous matrix. The corresponding contents of the three elements are presented in Table S1. An ultrahigh N-doped content, 15.26 wt% at 600 °C, was obtained for the N-PHCS. In addition, a high N-doped content at a higher temperature, 5.77 wt% at 900 °C, was also achieved, indicating N atoms remained within the carbon framework at high temperatures.

The XPS spectrum of C1s shown in Fig. S12 was deconvoluted into the following three peaks: 287.7 (CO/CO), 286.1 (CN/CN) and 285.0 eV (CC/CC). The XPS N1s spectrum of N-PHCS-600 presented in Fig. 8 was deconvoluted into the following three peaks: 398.32 (pyridinic N), 399.9 (pyrrolic N) and 401.0 eV (graphitic-type quaternary N). The high pyridinic and pyrrolic N contents of N-PHCS-600 (total 9.44 wt%) are presented in Table S1. However, an unusual peak at 297.30 eV, shown in Fig. 8a, indicated an incomplete transition from organic HEHSs into a carbon-based framework at 600 °C had occurred. At higher carbonization temperatures, N-doped carbon-based frameworks formed completely. The relative pyridinic, pyrrolic and total N contents continuously decreased as the temperature increased, as shown in Fig. 8b–d. The relative graphitic-type quaternary N content increased, which is in good agreement with previously reported work (Su et al., 2019). The elemental composition of the N-PHCS is presented in Table 1 . The elemental analysis and XPS results showed the N-PHCS were composed of the following elements: C, N, O and H. The highest N content of N-PHCS-600 achieved was 19.54 wt%, and the atomic ratio of C/N was near 3.75, which corresponded well with the XPS results (15.26 wt% at 600 °C).

Considering the N-rich properties of energetic precursors (primarily involved in N-containing energetic groups and bridging piperazine N), HEHSs were determined to be the key self-supplied N source for the formation of N-PHCS. Because the thermal decomposition of energetic functional groups (Nitro groups) occurs at high temperatures. N-containing gases, such as NO and NO2, were generated. Subsequently, these acidic gases could react with the carbon matrix to form N-doped carbon-based materials. In addition, the N-containing piperazine skeleton can be converted into a N-doped carbon-based framework. In this study, the carbon content increased as the N content decreased when the carbonization temperature was increased from 600 to 900 °C. However, the N content of N-PHCS at 900 °C remained relatively high at 6.10 wt%.

The N adsorption and desorption isotherms of the energetic precursor HEHSs and N-PHCS are shown in Fig. S13, and the SBET and SLangmuir data are presented in Table 1. Compared with the N adsorption isotherm of HEHSs, all the N-PHCS samples exhibited a steep increase at relatively low pressures (P/P0 < 0.001), indicative of a microporous structure. A steep increase at high pressures (P/P0 = 1) could be attributed to the presence of macropores formed from broken surfaces and defects. In addition, the value of SBET increased from 6.5 m2/g (HEHSs) to 236.0 m2/g (N-PHCS-600) and 503.5 m2/g (N-PHCS-900), demonstrating the carbon-based frameworks of these samples contained a large number of pores. The PSD of N-PHCS presented in Fig. S14 shows the presence of micropores, mesopores and macropores in the carbon-based frameworks, although the particle sizes were small. The mesopore size distribution of N-PHCS-600 was primarily between 11.3 and 19.6 cm. Porous structures were formed by N-rich gases generated by the decomposition of energetic functional groups in the matrix when exposed to high temperatures. Moreover, for N-PHCS-900, the primary mesoporous structure contained pore sizes of 11.4 and 19.8 nm within the carbon-based framework. Both microporous and macroporous pores were generated when the temperature was increased to 900 °C because the carbon-based framework continuously decomposed as the temperature increased and gases were produced and escaped. These results demonstrated high-temperature carbonization of the energetic precursor HEHSs produced carbon-based frameworks with a hierarchical porous structure.
The differences between and advantages of this strategy and previous reported methods were analyzed with regard to their safety risks and economic benefits.

The recycling and utilization of EMs for the fabrication of advanced materials is an emerging field (Dolmatov et al., 2013). However, its development is still in the initial stages (Liu et al., 2015b; Yang and Li, 2018). Several suitable methods based on the energy-releasing effects of EMs have been proposed, and several types of novel functional materials based on different energetic precursors have been prepared. These methods are reported in Table 2 . As mentioned previously, the primary preparation strategies are detonation and deflagration. High risks (extremely high temperatures and pressures) are associated with detonation and deflagration because of their intrinsic properties, especially when using high-energy organic explosives and energetic salts. These methods have been tested in a laboratory setting, resulting in low yields. But no industrial production has yet been attempted. In addition, high-temperature physical vapor deposition (PVD) methods were used to fabricate carbon-based functional materials. The pyrolysis temperature of the explosive 1,3,5-triamino-2,4,6-trinitrobenzene (TATB) can be controlled below 1000 °C in harsh conditions when a sealing system filled with a reductive atmosphere (N2 and H2) is used. However, with the present of explosive gas H2, complicated and rigorous experimental procedures increase the probability of dangerous accidents. Finally, industrialization is difficult because of the high cost and small preparation scale of traditional methods.

In comparison with traditional methods, this high-temperature carbonization strategy using HEHSs as energetic precursors to fabricate N-PHCS avoids high-risk behaviors because the energetic properties (energy release rates and intensities) of the energetic precursors are significantly reduced because of the co-polymerization reaction used. In addition, the simple carbonization conditions and high security of this method promote the possibility of its use in industrial applications.

A comparison of this method with traditional methods is presented in Table 3 . A larger amount of resources is consumed when using traditional methods for the preparation of N-doped carbonaceous materials, which include N-containing gas (NH3) and solid (melamine). However, the results clearly show the doping efficiency of traditional methods is not sufficiently high. Complicated process conditions, long periods and low yields usually limit the fabrication and application of N-doped hollow carbon nanospheres. In addition, the large-scale preparation of N-PHCS with a high N-doped content, good monodispersity and uniformity has been a formidable challenge.

The high-temperature carbonization strategy presented herein solved these problems associated with traditional methods, successfully recovered and utilized EMs and provided a green, clean and sustainable approach for converting high-energetic hazardous chemical resources into functional fundamental carbon-based materials. In addition, this sustainable strategy using HEHSs as energetic precursors had many advantages. (1) This method is a green, clean and sustainable route that reuses hazardous chemical resources reasonably and effectively. (2) Self-supplied N atoms derived from a N-rich energetic matrix can be doped into the carbon-based framework more efficiently and ultrahigh N-doped contents can be obtained easier than when using traditional methods. (3) Hollow carbon nanospheres with abundant hierarchical porous nanostructures were obtained, resulting in N-PHCS with a high surface area.

In summary, N-PHCS with a high N-doped content and high surface area were synthesized successfully using a one-step high-temperature carbonization strategy and HEHSs as energetic precursors.
(1)
Carbon-based hollow sphere nanostructures were obtained via the thermal decomposition of energetic precursors. The matrix was doped with self-supplied N, resulting in a high N-doped content at a carbonization temperature of 600 °C, 19.54 wt%, and even a N-doped content of 6.10 wt% at 900 °C. The obtained N-PHCS possessed a large amount of hierarchical pores and a surface area of 503.5 m2/g at 900 °C.
(2)
In comparison with the traditional disposal method of obsolete EMs, this green, clean and sustainable strategy demonstrated a cleaner method for the recovery and utilization of EMs by converting hazardous chemical resources into novel fundamental functional materials.
This work presented a new path for designing and fabricating sustainable carbon-based functional materials using obsolete EMs.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Appendix A
Electronic supplementary information.
Appendix A
Appendix B
The 3D simulation videos of N-PHCS-600.
Appendix B
Appendix C
The 3D simulation videos of N-PHCS-900.
Appendix C
Electronic supplementary information and 3D simulation videos related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.136725.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest. The founding sponsors had no role in the design of the study; in the collection, analyses, or interpretation of data; in the writing of the manuscript, and in the decision to publish the results.

This work was designed by Hui Ren (the 3rd author) and Qingjie Jiao (the 2nd author). The work was completed by Peng Deng (the first author). The manuscript was analyzed, written and revised by Peng Deng. This work is original research work. No conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (21975227 and 21975024). We thank BL07W in NSRL and Prof. Liang Chen (University of science and technology of China, Hefei, China), Prof. Yu Liu and Dr. Jinjiang Xu (Institute of chemical materials, China academy of engineering physics, Mianyang, China) for support and help.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cleanup of Chemical and Explosive Munitions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Albright, D. R., 2012. Cleanup of Chemical and Explosive Munitions, 2nd ed. Elsevier, Netherlands.
